title: Emergency Guide: Dog Large Open Wound Exposing Muscle
meta_desc: If your dog has a wound exposing muscle, this is a high-urgency emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is critical and how to help your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: A deep tissue wound exposing muscle is a critical veterinary emergency requiring immediate medical attention to prevent sepsis and permanent damage. Sending a photo of the injury to the clinic while in transit can help the veterinary team prepare for faster stabilization and treatment.

What are the characteristics and causes of a deep tissue wound in pets?

A wound that is deep enough to expose underlying muscle tissue is considered a severe, full-thickness injury. These injuries often result from significant trauma, such as animal attacks, car accidents, or contact with sharp objects. When the protective barrier of the skin is completely breached, the underlying muscle, nerves, and blood vessels are left vulnerable to debris, bacteria, and rapid dehydration.

Why is a deep tissue wound considered a high-level veterinary emergency?

  • Seek immediate medical attention; this is a critical emergency that should not wait for an appointment or overnight observation.
  • Wounds that expose muscle carry an extremely high risk of infection, sepsis, and permanent tissue damage.
  • Professional intervention is required to clean the wound, perform surgical closure or stitches, and manage pain and inflammation safely.

How does sending a photo help the emergency vet triage my pet's injury?

  • Sending a clear photo while in transit allows the triage staff to assess injury severity and estimate blood loss.
  • Advance visual information helps the team prepare the necessary surgical or stabilization equipment before your arrival.
  • This proactive communication ensures your pet receives the fastest possible care once you reach the facility.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of large open wounds exposing muscle in dogs, initial assessment involves a brief physical examination, noting lameness if the wound is on a limb. The wound's location, hemorrhage, configuration, and penetration of body cavities or synovial structures should be evaluated after applying sterile lubricant, clipping hair, sterile skin preparation, and lavage. Wounds exposing or penetrating bone or involving joints and tendons require thorough exploration for underlying structural damage. Dog bite wounds often have extensive tissue damage beneath the surface, necessitating surgical extension of the wound for proper examination and debridement before repair decisions are made; tension-relieving sutures, tissue flaps, or grafts may be needed. Damage to underlying structures like muscles, tendons, and blood vessels must be addressed before closure, and grossly contaminated wounds may require closure with drains or treatment as an open wound.

Chapter: Emergency, General Principles

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1716)

faqs.question: Should I try to clean a wound that exposes muscle at home?
faqs.answer: No. Deep wounds should not be flushed with hydrogen peroxide or home tap water, as this can damage sensitive muscle tissue. Simply cover the area loosely with a clean, dry towel and head to a vet immediately.

faqs.question: My dog is not bleeding heavily; is it still an emergency?
faqs.answer: Yes. While heavy bleeding is an additional concern, the depth of the wound itself is the emergency. Exposed muscle tissue will begin to die (necrosis) and become infected very quickly, regardless of the amount of blood visible.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain medication for the wound?
faqs.answer: Never give your dog human medications like ibuprofen, aspirin, or Tylenol, as these can be toxic or cause life-threatening complications. Your veterinarian will provide safe, fast-acting pain relief upon arrival.
